task.title: Configuring an IPsec host-to-host VPN with PSK authentication and separate data and control planes by using the vpn RHEL system role

li.p: You have prepared the control node and the managed nodes.
The account you use to connect to the managed nodes has sudo permissions for these nodes.

context.p: Use the vpn RHEL system role to automate the process of creating an IPsec host-to-host VPN. To enhance security by minimizing the risk of control messages being intercepted or disrupted, configure separate connections for both the data traffic and the control traffic.
A host-to-host VPN establishes a direct, secure, and encrypted connection between two devices, allowing applications to communicate safely over an insecure network, such as the internet.

For authentication, a pre-shared key (PSK) is a straightforward method that uses a single, shared secret known only to the two peers. This approach is simple to configure and ideal for basic setups where ease of deployment is a priority. However, you must keep the key strictly confidential. An attacker with access to the key can compromise the connection.

step.cmd: Create a playbook file, for example, ~/playbook.yml, with the following content:

info.codeblock: --- - name: Configuring VPN hosts: managed-node-01.example.com, managed-node-02.example.com tasks: - name: IPsec VPN with PSK authentication ansible.builtin.include_role: name: redhat.rhel_system_roles.vpn vars: vpn_connections: - name: control_plane_vpn hosts: managed-node-01.example.com: hostname: 203.0.113.1 # IP address for the control plane managed-node-02.example.com: hostname: 198.51.100.2 # IP address for the control plane auth_method: psk auto: start - name: data_plane_vpn hosts: managed-node-01.example.com: hostname: 10.0.0.1 # IP address for the data plane managed-node-02.example.com: hostname: 172.16.0.2 # IP address for the data plane auth_method: psk auto: start vpn_manage_firewall: true vpn_manage_selinux: true
info.p: The settings specified in the example playbook include the following:

dd.p: Defines a YAML dictionary with the hosts between which you want to configure a VPN. The connections are named <name>-<IP_address_A>-to-<IP_address_B> , for example control_plane_vpn-203.0.113.1-to-198.51.100.2.
The role configures the VPN connection on each managed node. Note that the role cannot configure Libreswan on external (unmanaged) nodes. You must manually create the configuration on these hosts.
Enables PSK authentication between the hosts. The role uses openssl on the control node to create the pre-shared key.

Specifies the startup method of the connection. Valid values are add, ondemand, start, and ignore. For details, see the ipsec.conf(5) man page on a system with Libreswan installed. The default value of this variable is null, which means no automatic startup operation.
Defines that the role opens the required ports in the firewalld service on the managed nodes.
Defines that the role sets the required SELinux port type on the IPsec ports.

info.p: For details about all variables used in the playbook, see the /usr/share/ansible/roles/rhel-system-roles.vpn/README.md file on the control node.
Note that this command only validates the syntax and does not protect against a wrong but valid configuration.

li.p: Confirm that the connections are successfully started, for example:
li.codeblock: # ansible managed-node-01.example.com -m shell -a 'ipsec trafficstatus | grep "control_plane_vpn-203.0.113.1-to-198.51.100.2"' ... 006 #3: "control_plane_vpn-203.0.113.1-to-198.51.100.2", type=ESP, add_time=1741860073, inBytes=0, outBytes=0, maxBytes=2^63B, id='198.51.100.2'
li.p: Note that this command only succeeds if the VPN connection is active. If you set the auto variable in the playbook to a value other than start, you might need to manually activate the connection on the managed nodes first.
